name: band straight leg deadlift
instructions: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and place the band around your feet.
Hold the band with both hands, palms facing your body, and keep your arms straight.
Engage your core and maintain a slight bend in your knees.
Slowly hinge forward at your hips, keeping your back straight and chest lifted.
Lower the band towards the ground while keeping your legs straight.
Pause for a moment at the bottom, then squeeze your glutes and hamstrings to return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and place the band around your feet. Hold the band with both hands, palms facing your body, and keep your arms straight. Engage your core and maintain a slight bend in your knees. Slowly hinge forward at your hips, keeping your back straight and chest lifted. Lower the band towards the ground while keeping your legs straight. Pause for a moment at the bottom, then squeeze your glutes and hamstrings to return to the starting position. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

conditionNotes.note: Keep load symmetrical and stop at positions that create sharp pain or twisting.
Use pain-free range of motion, slower tempo, and lighter progressions when joints feel irritated.
Weight-bearing resistance exercises are beneficial for bone density when load is appropriate.
Avoid loaded spinal flexion and excessive hinge depth. Brace core and use supported positions.
Maintain consistent breathing throughout. Avoid breath-holding.
Both aerobic and resistance training improve insulin sensitivity. Monitor blood sugar before, during, and after sessions. Have a fast-acting carb available.
Rhythm-based exercises and large-amplitude movements are beneficial. Focus on motor confidence.
Focus on muscle co-contraction and joint stability. Avoid locking out joints passively.
Functional patterns are generally safe. Prioritize pelvic floor awareness and avoid Valsalva.
Start light with higher reps and shorter ROM. Progress load gradually as pain-free capacity increases.
